title: Drug Allergy & Hypersensitivity
objectives: Classify drug hypersensitivity reactions using the Gell-Coombs system
Distinguish immediate (Type I) from delayed (Type IV) drug reactions
Recognize severe cutaneous adverse reactions (SCARs): SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP
Apply risk stratification for penicillin allergy evaluation and delabeling
Understand cross-reactivity patterns among beta-lactam antibiotics
Identify contraindications to drug challenge and desensitization

guidelineReferences.name: AAAAI/ACAAI Drug Allergy Practice Parameter 2022
guidelineReferences.citation: Khan DA, et al. Drug allergy: A 2022 practice parameter update. J Allergy Clin Immunol. 2022;150:1333-1393
keyPoints: Penicillin allergy is reported by 10% but true allergy <1%
Low-risk patients can undergo direct oral challenge without skin testing
SCARs are contraindication to rechallenge and desensitization
80% of IgE-mediated penicillin allergy wanes after 10 years

guidelineReferences.name: PMC: Drug Hypersensitivity Diagnosis and Prevention
guidelineReferences.citation: Böhm R, Cascorbi I. Drug Hypersensitivity: Diagnosis, Genetics, and Prevention. Dtsch Arztebl Int. 2018;115:501-512

vignette.text:
A 45-year-old woman is referred to allergy clinic with a reported 'penicillin allergy.' She states that at age 12, she developed a rash while taking amoxicillin for an ear infection. She cannot recall details but thinks it was 'hives.' She has avoided all penicillins since then. She now needs a dental procedure and her dentist wants to give amoxicillin prophylaxis for her prosthetic heart valve.

Past Medical History: Prosthetic aortic valve (bicuspid valve replacement 5 years ago), Hypertension, GERD

Medications: Lisinopril 10mg daily, Omeprazole 20mg daily, Warfarin 5mg daily

Allergies: Penicillin (rash at age 12, ~33 years ago)

Social History: Non-smoker, social alcohol

additionalHistory.originalReaction: Rash (patient recalls 'bumps') during amoxicillin course at age 12
additionalHistory.otherSymptoms: No respiratory symptoms, no swelling, no anaphylaxis features
additionalHistory.subsequentExposure: Has taken cephalosporins (cephalexin for UTI 5 years ago) without reaction
additionalHistory.familyHistory: No family history of drug allergy

questions.stem: You are reviewing the classification of drug hypersensitivity reactions.
questions.question: What is the Gell-Coombs classification of hypersensitivity reactions?
questions.hint: Consider the immune mechanism and timing for each type.

options.text: Only Type I reactions are drug allergies
Type IV reactions occur within minutes
All drug reactions are IgE-mediated

Type I: IgE-mediated immediate (urticaria, anaphylaxis); Type II: IgG/IgM cytotoxic (hemolytic anemia); Type III: Immune complex (serum sickness); Type IV: T-cell mediated delayed (contact dermatitis, DRESS, SJS/TEN)

questions.explanation: Gell-Coombs classification: Type I (Immediate): IgE-mediated, mast cell degranulation, occurs within minutes to hours - urticaria, angioedema, anaphylaxis. Type II (Cytotoxic): IgG/IgM antibodies against cell-surface antigens - drug-induced hemolytic anemia, thrombocytopenia. Type III (Immune complex): IgG/IgM immune complex deposition - serum sickness, vasculitis. Type IV (Delayed/T-cell): T-cell mediated, occurs hours to weeks - contact dermatitis, maculopapular rash, DRESS, SJS/TEN, AGEP.

whyChain.answer: Immediate reactions (<1-6 hours) are typically IgE-mediated (Type I) and carry anaphylaxis risk. Delayed reactions (>24 hours to weeks) are usually T-cell mediated (Type IV). This affects evaluation approach - skin testing works for Type I but not Type IV; provocation testing timing differs.

whyChain.why: Which type was this patient's childhood reaction?
whyChain.answer: Her reaction (rash appearing days into treatment) suggests a delayed Type IV reaction, likely maculopapular exanthem - the most common drug reaction. This is different from IgE-mediated urticaria and is generally lower risk for severe reaction on re-exposure.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters. Drug allergy: an updated practice parameter. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2010;105(4):259-273. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: This patient reports penicillin allergy but the reaction was 33 years ago.
questions.question: What percentage of patients reporting penicillin allergy have true IgE-mediated allergy, and why?
questions.hint: Consider the natural history of penicillin allergy.

options.text: 90% have true allergy confirmed on testing
50% have true allergy
<10% have true allergy on testing; 80% of true IgE-mediated allergy wanes within 10 years; many 'allergies' were never allergic (viral exanthem, intolerance, family history)
Penicillin allergy is lifelong and never resolves

questions.explanation: Penicillin allergy is reported by ~10% of population, but <10% (often <5%) have true allergy when formally tested. Reasons: (1) 80% of true IgE-mediated allergy wanes within 10 years - IgE has short half-life; (2) Original reaction was often viral exanthem misattributed to antibiotic; (3) Reaction was intolerance (GI upset, headache), not allergy; (4) Label inherited from family history. This patient's reaction 33 years ago is very unlikely to represent current allergy.

whyChain.answer: Penicillin allergy label causes: (1) Use of broader-spectrum, more expensive antibiotics; (2) Increased C. difficile and resistant organism risk; (3) Surgical site infection rates; (4) Longer hospital stays. Delabeling saves lives and healthcare costs.

whyChain.why: What if the original reaction was true anaphylaxis?
whyChain.answer: Even true anaphylaxis often wanes over time. History of anaphylaxis warrants more careful evaluation (skin testing before challenge) but doesn't preclude delabeling. However, very recent (<5 years) severe reactions require more caution.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are risk-stratifying this patient's reported penicillin allergy.
questions.question: What makes a penicillin allergy history 'low-risk' vs 'high-risk'?
questions.hint: Consider the type and timing of original reaction.

options.text: All penicillin allergies are high-risk

Low-risk: remote reaction (>10 years), non-severe symptoms (isolated rash, GI symptoms), unknown/vague history, tolerated penicillin since. High-risk: recent anaphylaxis, severe reaction (intubation, ICU), positive skin test, recurrent reactions
Age at reaction determines risk
Only family history determines risk

questions.explanation: Risk stratification guides evaluation approach. LOW-RISK: Reaction >10 years ago, isolated non-severe rash, GI symptoms only (nausea, diarrhea), vague/unknown history, family history only, subsequent tolerance of penicillin class. HIGH-RISK: Recent (<5 years) reaction, anaphylaxis features (hypotension, airway involvement), required ICU/intubation, positive prior skin test, SCAR history (SJS/TEN, DRESS). This patient is LOW-RISK (33 years ago, vague rash, tolerated cephalosporin).

whyChain.why: Can low-risk patients skip skin testing?
whyChain.answer: Yes - current guidelines support direct oral challenge (without skin testing) for low-risk patients. Multiple studies show this is safe with <2% reaction rate, mostly mild. This expands access to delabeling beyond allergy specialists.
whyChain.source: JACI: Safe Penicillin Delabeling in Primary Care

whyChain.answer: She tolerated cephalexin 5 years ago. This is strong evidence against current penicillin allergy - if she had true IgE-mediated penicillin allergy, there's ~2% cross-reactivity with cephalosporins (higher with similar side chains). Cephalosporin tolerance essentially rules out significant penicillin allergy.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are planning to evaluate this low-risk patient.
questions.question: What is the recommended approach for penicillin allergy evaluation in low-risk patients?
questions.hint: Consider whether skin testing is always required.

options.text: Direct oral amoxicillin challenge without skin testing is safe and effective for low-risk patients; give full therapeutic dose (250-500mg) and observe 1 hour; can be done by non-allergists with proper training
Skin testing required for all patients
Penicillin allergy cannot be safely evaluated
Only allergists can evaluate penicillin allergy

questions.explanation: For LOW-RISK patients: Direct oral challenge (DOC) without skin testing is recommended. Protocol: Give amoxicillin 250-500mg, observe 60 minutes for immediate reaction, then discharge with instructions to report delayed reactions. Success rate >95% for delabeling. Can be performed by trained non-allergists (pharmacists, primary care, inpatient teams). For HIGH-RISK: Skin testing first, then graded challenge if negative.

whyChain.why: What if she has a reaction during challenge?
whyChain.answer: Reactions during low-risk DOC are uncommon (<2%) and usually mild (urticaria, pruritus). Treat with antihistamines; anaphylaxis is extremely rare. Have epinephrine available but rarely needed. If reaction occurs, patient remains labeled allergic.

Document penicillin allergy as 'disproved' or 'delabeled' in medical record. Provide patient with written documentation. Counsel on the meaning of delabeling. She can then safely receive amoxicillin prophylaxis.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: The patient asks about using cephalosporins if she can't take penicillin.
questions.question: What is the cross-reactivity between penicillins and cephalosporins?
questions.hint: Consider the structural basis of cross-reactivity.

options.text: No cross-reactivity exists
Only IV cephalosporins cross-react
100% cross-reactivity - never give cephalosporins

True cross-reactivity is ~2% (not the historical 10%); highest with shared R1 side chains (ampicillin-cephalexin, amoxicillin-cefadroxil); cephalosporins with different side chains can often be used safely

questions.explanation: Historical 10% cross-reactivity was overestimated (contaminated early cephalosporins). Actual cross-reactivity ~2% and driven by R1 side chain similarity, not the shared beta-lactam ring. High cross-reactivity pairs (same R1): ampicillin-cephalexin-cefadroxil; amoxicillin-cefadroxil-cefprozil. Low cross-reactivity: ceftriaxone, cefepime, ceftazidime (different side chains). Carbapenems have ~1% cross-reactivity.

whyChain.why: Can this patient safely take cephalosporins?
whyChain.answer: She already did! She tolerated cephalexin without reaction. This proves she doesn't have clinically significant cross-reactivity. Even without that history, a patient with remote, vague penicillin allergy can safely receive cephalosporins with different side chains.

Cross-reactivity with carbapenems is ~1% - even lower than cephalosporins. Carbapenems can be safely given to most penicillin-allergic patients, especially those with low-risk histories. Skin testing or graded challenge can be done for high-risk patients who need carbapenems.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A different patient reports a severe reaction to lamotrigine with fever, rash, and liver involvement.
questions.question: What are the Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions (SCARs) and why are they important?

options.text: Rechallenge is safe after SCAR
SCARs are mild reactions that resolve spontaneously

SCARs include SJS/TEN (skin sloughing, mucosal involvement, mortality 10-30%), DRESS (fever, rash, eosinophilia, organ involvement), AGEP (pustules, fever); all are Type IV reactions and contraindication to rechallenge
SCARs are IgE-mediated

questions.explanation: Severe Cutaneous Adverse Reactions (SCARs) are life-threatening Type IV hypersensitivity reactions: (1) SJS/TEN: Blistering, epidermal detachment (<10% SJS, 10-30% overlap, >30% TEN), mucosal involvement, Nikolsky sign, mortality 10-30%; (2) DRESS: Fever, extensive rash, eosinophilia, lymphadenopathy, organ involvement (liver, kidney, heart), latency 2-8 weeks; (3) AGEP: Acute pustular eruption, fever, neutrophilia. ALL SCARs are absolute contraindications to rechallenge with culprit drug - can be fatal on re-exposure.

whyChain.why: How is DRESS different from simple drug rash?
whyChain.answer: DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) involves systemic inflammation: fever, eosinophilia (often >1500), lymphadenopathy, and internal organ involvement (hepatitis most common, also nephritis, pneumonitis, carditis). Associated with HHV-6 reactivation. Mortality 5-10%. Requires systemic steroids.

whyChain.why: What HLA associations exist for SCARs?
whyChain.answer: Important pharmacogenomic associations: HLA-B*57:01 → abacavir hypersensitivity (screen before use); HLA-B*15:02 → carbamazepine SJS/TEN (screen in Asian populations); HLA-B*58:01 → allopurinol SCAR. Pre-prescription HLA testing prevents these reactions.
whyChain.source: PMC: Updated Review of Drug Hypersensitivity

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: You are teaching residents to recognize SJS/TEN.
questions.question: What are the clinical features and red flags for SJS/TEN?
questions.hint: Consider the skin findings and timeline.

options.text: Immediate urticaria without mucosal involvement
Never involves mucous membranes
Occurs within minutes of drug exposure

Prodrome (fever, malaise) → painful skin → targetoid lesions → blistering/epidermal detachment → mucosal involvement (oral, ocular, genital); Nikolsky sign positive; onset usually 1-3 weeks after drug initiation

questions.explanation: SJS/TEN clinical features: (1) Prodrome 1-3 days: fever, malaise, upper respiratory symptoms; (2) Skin pain often precedes visible rash; (3) Atypical targetoid/purpuric macules → blisters → sheet-like epidermal detachment; (4) Mucosal involvement >90% (oral erosions, conjunctivitis - can cause blindness, genital); (5) Nikolsky sign: lateral pressure causes skin sloughing; (6) Onset typically 1-3 weeks after starting culprit drug. Common culprits: sulfonamides, anticonvulsants, allopurinol, NSAIDs, antibiotics.

whyChain.answer: By body surface area (BSA) of epidermal detachment: SJS <10% BSA, SJS-TEN overlap 10-30%, TEN >30%. SCORTEN score predicts mortality based on age, malignancy, BSA, BUN, bicarbonate, glucose, heart rate. TEN mortality approaches 30%.

Immediate: Stop ALL suspect drugs. Supportive care in burn unit/ICU. Fluid resuscitation, wound care, prevent infection. Ophthalmology consult (prevent corneal scarring). Role of IVIG, cyclosporine, etanercept debated but often used. NO rechallenge with culprit drug - ever.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: A patient with confirmed penicillin allergy (positive skin test) needs penicillin G for neurosyphilis.
questions.question: When is drug desensitization indicated and what are the contraindications?
questions.hint: Consider when there's no alternative and what reactions preclude desensitization.

options.text: SCARs can be safely desensitized
Desensitization is permanent

Indicated when: drug is essential AND no alternatives exist (e.g., penicillin for neurosyphilis, aspirin for cardiac stents). Contraindicated in: SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP), hemolytic anemia, serum sickness, organ-specific reactions (hepatitis, nephritis)
Desensitization works for all drug allergies

questions.explanation: Desensitization is temporary tolerance induction for IgE-mediated reactions when: (1) Drug is essential (no alternatives), AND (2) Patient has confirmed or high-risk allergy. Examples: Penicillin for syphilis in pregnancy, chemotherapy agents, aspirin for cardiac disease. CONTRAINDICATIONS: SCARs (SJS/TEN, DRESS, AGEP) - can be fatal on re-exposure; serum sickness; drug-induced hemolytic anemia; organ-specific reactions. Desensitization is NOT permanent - tolerance lasts only while drug is continued.

whyChain.answer: Mechanism: Gradual dose escalation (doubling doses every 15-30 min) causes mast cell hyporesponsiveness without triggering full degranulation. Starts with 1/10,000th to 1/1,000,000th of therapeutic dose. Takes 4-12 hours. Must be done in monitored setting with resuscitation capability.

Neurosyphilis requires penicillin G - no acceptable alternative (ceftriaxone has inferior CNS penetration). If skin test positive, proceed with IV penicillin desensitization protocol, then immediately begin therapeutic dosing. Maintain continuous penicillin - if interrupted >24-48 hours, must re-desensitize.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.

questions.stem: Returning to the original patient with remote penicillin 'allergy' needing amoxicillin prophylaxis.
questions.question: What is the appropriate evaluation and management for this patient?
questions.hint: Apply risk stratification and determine if delabeling is appropriate.

options.text: Avoid penicillin forever, use clindamycin
Requires full skin testing and desensitization
Cannot evaluate - send to ER for any antibiotic

Low-risk history (33 years ago, vague rash, tolerated cephalosporin); proceed with direct oral amoxicillin challenge without skin testing; if tolerates, delabel and provide amoxicillin prophylaxis for dental procedure

questions.explanation: This patient has LOW-RISK penicillin allergy: (1) Remote reaction (33 years), (2) Vague symptoms (rash, not anaphylaxis), (3) Tolerated cephalosporin since (essentially proves no significant cross-reactive allergy). Management: Direct oral challenge with amoxicillin 500mg, observe 60 minutes. If tolerates (expected >95%), delabel penicillin allergy from record and provide documentation. She can then safely receive amoxicillin for dental prophylaxis. This is safer than using alternatives like clindamycin (C. diff risk).

whyChain.why: Why not just use clindamycin to be 'safe'?
whyChain.answer: Clindamycin alternatives carry real risks: higher C. difficile rates, less effective for some indications, promotes resistance. The 'safe' choice of avoiding penicillin actually causes more harm than proper evaluation. Delabeling improves patient outcomes.

After successful challenge: (1) Update medical record to remove/clarify penicillin allergy; (2) Give written documentation stating 'penicillin allergy evaluated [date] - tolerated amoxicillin challenge - allergy disproved'; (3) Counsel patient to share this with future providers.

sources.text: StatPearls: Drug Allergy. NCBI Bookshelf. Accessed January 14, 2026.
Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters. Drug allergy: an updated practice parameter. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol. 2010;105(4):259-273. Accessed January 14, 2026.

mcqs.stem: Which of the following is the MOST important initial step in evaluating a patient with suspected drug allergy?

options.text: Obtain a thorough history and physical examination
options.explanation: A comprehensive history and physical examination is the foundation of clinical reasoning in drug allergy. It guides all subsequent diagnostic and management decisions.

options.text: Order advanced imaging immediately
options.explanation: Advanced imaging may be indicated but should be guided by clinical findings, not ordered reflexively.

options.text: Start empiric treatment before evaluation
options.explanation: Treatment should generally follow an appropriate diagnostic evaluation unless the patient is critically unstable.

options.text: Consult a specialist before any workup
options.explanation: Initial evaluation should be performed by the treating physician; specialist consultation follows based on findings.

mcqs.stem: A patient presents with classic features of drug allergy. Which clinical reasoning principle is MOST important in avoiding diagnostic error?

options.text: Search satisficing - stop looking after the first diagnosis
options.explanation: Premature closure (stopping the diagnostic process too early) is a common cognitive error that leads to missed diagnoses.

options.text: Consider the differential diagnosis systematically
options.explanation: Systematic consideration of differential diagnoses, including must-not-miss conditions, is essential for avoiding premature closure and anchoring bias.

options.text: Rely primarily on pattern recognition
options.explanation: While pattern recognition (System 1 thinking) is useful, it should be balanced with analytical reasoning (System 2) to avoid cognitive biases.

options.text: Order every available test to rule out all possibilities
options.explanation: Indiscriminate testing leads to false positives, unnecessary procedures, and increased cost without improving diagnostic accuracy.

mcqs.stem: When managing drug allergy, which approach best supports evidence-based practice?

options.text: Follow institutional protocols regardless of patient factors
options.explanation: Evidence-based practice integrates best evidence with clinical expertise AND patient values/circumstances.

options.text: Base decisions solely on clinical experience
options.explanation: Clinical experience is valuable but can introduce bias; it should be combined with current evidence.

options.text: Integrate current best evidence with clinical expertise and patient values
options.explanation: Evidence-based medicine is the conscientious integration of best research evidence, clinical expertise, and patient values in making clinical decisions.

options.text: Always defer to the most recent published study
options.explanation: Individual studies should be appraised for quality and relevance; systematic reviews and guidelines generally provide stronger evidence.

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.
